articles.excerpt: The State has yet to fully recover almost €90m in overpayments made during the pandemic, new figures released to the Sunday Independent show.
articles.content:
More than 50m recouped so far as minister says team is working to chase outstanding PUP debt

articles.title: Government to spend €500k planting trees to offset Ryder Cup emissions
articles.excerpt: The State is to spend €500,000 planting trees in a bid to offset the carbon emissions generated by the Ryder Cup golf tournament being held at Adare Manor in Co Limerick next year.
articles.content:

articles.title: ‘He’s willing to push boundaries’: Kneecap manager Daniel Lambert’s provocative playbook, from Bohs to Iran
articles.excerpt: As Micheál Martin was preparing for his White House encounter with Donald Trump last week, Kneecap announced they were bound for Cuba, an island “being strangled by the man who’ll be handed the shamrock”.
articles.content: As Micheál Martin was preparing for his White House encounter with Donald Trump last week, Kneecap announced they were bound for Cuba, an island “being strangled by the man who’ll be handed the shamrock”.

articles.title: Shane Ross: My father almost completely cut me out of his will as he felt I had lost the run of myself and become utterly obnoxious
articles.excerpt: Up until the time that my father, a comfortably off middle-class ­Dublin solicitor, died a few years ago, I was a bellicose opponent of inheritance tax.

articles.title: Paul Kearns: Invisible Palestinians and imaginary Iranians — how Israel’s media is presenting this war

articles.title: Letters: If we want children to read more, schools need to scale back on screen time
articles.excerpt: Madam — Julia Molony highlights the decline in reading for pleasure (‘With fewer people reading for pleasure, should we be worried?’, March 15), but overlooks an important point: schools are increasingly pushing children towards screens rather than books.
articles.content: Madam — Julia Molony highlights the decline in reading for pleasure (‘With fewer people reading for pleasure, should we be worried?’, March 15), but overlooks an important point: schools are increasingly pushing children towards screens rather than books.
